questions.question: Which of these is the best alt text for a bar chart showing student enrollment by year?

options.text: Image of a chart with numbers on the axes
Bar chart showing enrollment increased from 500 students in 2018 to 750 in 2024, with the largest growth occurring between 2020 and 2022
A very detailed description of every single data point and number in the chart in a long paragraph

questions.explanation: Good alt text for a chart should summarize what the chart shows and highlight the key trend or insight (why it's in your course). It's not a generic description ('bar chart'), and it's not data overload (listing every number). The correct answer gives the trend (enrollment increased) and specific key points (from 500 to 750) that a student who can't see the chart would need to understand the main point. This allows a student using a screen reader to understand the data relationships.

questions.question: A faculty member uses a table to organize office hours in two columns for visual layout. What is the accessibility concern?

options.text: There is no accessibility concern; tables are always the best way to organize information
Screen readers expect rows and columns in tables to have semantic meaning; using a table just for layout confuses assistive technology users
Tables are too slow to load and will delay students from accessing office hour information
The table will not display on mobile devices

questions.explanation: Layout tables violate accessibility principles because screen readers read tables cell-by-cell, expecting each cell to be part of a coherent data structure. When a table is used purely for visual formatting, the structure doesn't match the semantic meaning, confusing users relying on assistive technology. For office hours, a bulleted list, paragraph formatting, or definition list is more appropriate and naturally accessible. If data actually needs table structure (like a grade book), then a table is the right choice.
